question.text: Which of the following best describes the 'carried interest' earned by an AIF investment manager?

answer.text: A share of the fund's profits above the hurdle rate, acting as a performance incentive

explanation.text: **The Logic:** Carried interest (carry) is the investment manager's share of profits above the hurdle rate — typically 20% in the '2-20' model. It aligns manager and investor interests by incentivizing the manager to generate returns above the benchmark. **The Trap:** Carry is earned only when the fund generates returns above the hurdle rate. Unlike management fees (fixed), carry is entirely performance-dependent. **Cross-Exam Context:** Carried interest structure is tested in XIX-A Chapter 3 and Chapter 6. **Distributor's Pro-Tip:** Carry is the main motivation for fund managers to outperform. A manager who has earned significant carry from prior funds has a proven ability to deliver above-hurdle returns — this is a strong track record indicator.
